input persona: A high school physics teacher who enjoys teaching students about the principles of flight and the mechanics of aircraft, and can use this example to illustrate the concepts of lift, thrust, and aerodynamics.
synthesized text: As I stood in front of my physics class, I held up a small model airplane to illustrate the concept of lift. 'So, class, can anyone tell me why this plane is able to fly?' I asked, scanning the room for a volunteer. 'Is it because of the wings?' one student ventured. 'That's correct!' I replied. 'The shape of the wing deflects the air downward, creating an area of lower air pressure above the wing and an area of higher air pressure below. This difference in pressure creates an upward force, or lift, that counteracts the weight of the plane and allows it to fly.' I then drew a diagram on the board to illustrate the concept of Bernoulli's principle and how it relates to lift. 'Now, can anyone tell me what would happen if we increased the angle of attack?' I asked, anticipating a lively discussion about stall speeds and aerodynamic drag.
label: instruction
